title: From domain adaptation to LLM benchmarking
text: My recent work on multilingual and retrieval-augmented evaluation did not start from nowhere. It grew out of an earlier question that has been consistent across projects: what happens when the assumptions made by training data and benchmark design stop matching the setting where a system is actually used?

title: From domain adaptation to LLM benchmarking
text:
Earlier Work: Generalization Under Shift
My earlier research focused on domain adaptation, event detection, and information extraction. In those projects, the main challenge was transfer: a model learned from one domain but was expected to work in another, often with different distributions, different vocabulary, or different evidence structure.
That line of work pushed me toward a broader view of robustness. Performance on a benchmark is only part of the story. The harder and more important question is how well the model behaves when the data, language, or task structure changes.

title: From domain adaptation to LLM benchmarking
section: Why the Same Question Shows Up in LLM Evaluation
text:
Why the Same Question Shows Up in LLM Evaluation
Large language models change the scale of the problem, but not the core issue. A benchmark can still be too narrow, too clean, or too easy to overfit conceptually. This is especially visible in multilingual evaluation and retrieval-augmented generation.
In multilingual settings, translation alone is not enough. Systems need to handle knowledge that is distributed differently across languages and cultures. In retrieval-augmented settings, success depends not only on generation quality but also on whether the evidence is relevant, reliable, and appropriately integrated.

title: From domain adaptation to LLM benchmarking
section: What I Want Benchmarks to Measure Better
text:
What I Want Benchmarks to Measure Better
There are three things I want new evaluations to capture more directly.

Robustness to noisy or misleading evidence.
Integration of diverse knowledge sources, especially across languages.
Reasoning behavior that becomes visible only when the task grows more complex.

These are the conditions where a system becomes useful or unreliable in practice. They are also the conditions that simpler benchmarks often wash away.

title: From domain adaptation to LLM benchmarking
text:
Where This Leads
This is why my current work emphasizes benchmark design for multilingual evaluation, medical RAG, and more demanding reasoning settings. The goal is not only to rank models, but to make evaluation informative enough to guide better systems.
That is the thread connecting my earlier research to my current direction: not a change in topic so much as a stronger commitment to testing models under the kinds of variation they will actually face.

title: CV
text:
Profile
Nghia Trung Ngo is a Ph.D. student in Computer Science at the University of Oregon. His research focuses on generalized learning settings, including domain adaptation, multilingual learning, multi-task learning, retrieval-augmented generation, and benchmark design for large language models.

Thesis: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Event Detection

title: CV
text:
Research Experience

Research Assistant, University of Oregon
2022 to present

Research topics include transfer learning, multilingual learning, and multi-task learning.
Developed work on joint learning for multiple information extraction tasks in domain adaptation settings.
Introduced generalized multi-transfer settings for cross-lingual learning.
Current directions include medical RAG benchmarking, multilingual knowledge integration, and complex commonsense reasoning benchmarks.



NLP Research Resident, VinAI Research
2020 to 2021

Worked on domain adaptation and event extraction.
Proposed adapter-based representation learning for unsupervised domain adaptation in event detection.
Contributed to an applied project benchmarking vision transformers on fisheye images for autonomous driving.



Research Student, Data Science Laboratory, HUST
2018 to 2019

Worked on machine learning foundations and probabilistic topic modeling.

title: Home
text:
NLP · Multilingual Learning · LLM Evaluation



Ph.D. student in Computer Science at the University of Oregon, advised by Prof. Thien Huu Nguyen.
I study learning and evaluation in generalized settings, with a focus on domain adaptation, multilingual learning, multi-task learning, and the behavior of large language models in practical environments. My recent work centers on multilingual benchmarks, retrieval-augmented generation, and evaluation settings that better reflect noisy, real-world use.

Download CV Research Overview Google Scholar

title: Home
text:
Research Themes

Domain adaptation for information extraction Multilingual and multi-task learning LLM benchmarking and evaluation Retrieval-augmented generation Robust AI systems in practical settings

title: Home
text:
Current Directions


Medical RAG Reliability
MedRGB is a benchmarking framework that evaluates medical RAG systems across four practical scenarios — Standard-RAG, Sufficiency, Integration, and Robustness — going beyond accuracy to assess reliability under noise, insufficient evidence, and adversarial misinformation.


Multilingual Knowledge Integration
My recent work examines how multilingual systems combine knowledge from distinct language and cultural sources, rather than treating translation as a sufficient evaluation proxy. This includes mSCoRe, a benchmark for skill-based commonsense reasoning across 5 languages and 10 reasoning skills.


Harder Reasoning Benchmarks
I am interested in benchmarks that scale with model capability. mSCoRe introduces dynamic complexity scaling (L0–L3) across 10 reasoning skills. ESV-RAG extends this to multi-hop QA through MCTS-guided Explore–Solve–Verify planning.

Read the blog

title: Research
text: My research focuses on machine learning and natural language processing under generalized and realistic conditions. Across projects, the common theme is robustness: how models transfer across domains, languages, tasks, and evidence conditions, and how we evaluate them when benchmarks no longer reflect real-world complexity.

title: Research
text: My research focuses on machine learning and natural language processing under generalized and realistic conditions. Across projects, the common theme is robustness: how models transfer across domains, languages, tasks, and evidence conditions, and how we evaluate them when benchmarks no longer reflect real-world complexity.

title: Research
section: Theme 1: Domain Adaptation for Information Extraction
text:
Theme 1: Domain Adaptation for Information Extraction
My earlier work focused on domain adaptation for event detection and joint information extraction. The goal was to improve performance when training and test data come from different domains, without assuming abundant labeled data in the target domain.
Representative directions:

Domain-specific adapters for event detection with layer-wise adversarial alignment and Wasserstein sample selection (DAA, Findings of ACL-IJCNLP 2021).
Meta self-paced learning for text classification under domain shift, integrating MAML-style meta-learning with dynamic curriculum selection (MSP-DA, COLING 2022).
Joint modeling for four information extraction tasks simultaneously under domain shift, using instance-relational adversarial training (DA4JIE, Findings of EMNLP 2022).

title: Research
text:
Theme 2: Multilingual and Multi-Task Learning
I am interested in transfer settings where the model must generalize across multiple source and target languages rather than a single source-target pair. This includes multilingual evaluation, language selection, and settings where transfer structure matters as much as scale.
Representative directions:

Cross-lingual transfer for information extraction with principled multi-source language selection via linguistic distance metrics and graph-relational domain adaptation (ZSRL, Findings of ACL 2023).
Multilingual datasets and benchmarks, including CulturaX (167 languages, LREC-COLING 2024) and ChatGPT Beyond English (37 languages, 7 tasks, Findings of EMNLP 2023).
Language-disentangled pre-training that explicitly separates language-specific and language-agnostic representations via knowledge distillation (LDD).
Federated learning for multilingual NLP with personalized tokenizers and semi-asynchronous tier training, reducing per-round communication payload by over 90% (MlFD).

title: Research
text:
Theme 3: LLM Benchmarking and Evaluation
Recent work has shifted toward evaluation for large language models, particularly where standard benchmarks are too clean or too narrow. I am interested in benchmarks that test integration, reasoning, and robustness rather than isolated point performance.
Current directions:

Multilingual commonsense reasoning with dynamic complexity scaling across 5 languages and 10 reasoning skills (mSCoRe, LREC-COLING 2026).
Multi-hop question answering via MCTS-guided Explore–Solve–Verify planning, where an agent selects adaptive action sequences based on question complexity (ESV-RAG, in progress).
Harder commonsense and task-based reasoning benchmarks that expose model limitations as task complexity increases.

title: Research
section: Theme 4: Retrieval-Augmented Systems in Practical Settings
text:
Theme 4: Retrieval-Augmented Systems in Practical Settings
Retrieval-augmented generation is often evaluated in idealized pipelines. I am interested in what happens when evidence is incomplete, noisy, or misleading, especially in domains such as medical question answering where reliability matters as much as accuracy.
What this theme emphasizes:

Evidence quality and trustworthiness across four practical test scenarios: Standard-RAG, Sufficiency, Integration, and Robustness (MedRGB, AAAI 2026 Workshop).
Robustness to noise and adversarial misinformation in retrieved documents.
Evaluation protocols that scale with model capability and signal-to-noise ratio.

title: Research
text:
Current Work Snapshot


Medical RAG Benchmarking
MedRGB evaluates medical RAG systems across four practical scenarios with varying proportions of signal and noise documents, assessing reliability and trustworthiness beyond accuracy alone.


Multilingual Commonsense Reasoning
mSCoRe introduces 5,600 multilingual skill-annotated commonsense questions with dynamic complexity scaling (L0–L3) across 10 reasoning skills and 5 languages.


MCTS-Guided Multi-hop QA
ESV-RAG formulates multi-hop question answering as a planning problem solved by Monte Carlo Tree Search, where an agent adaptively selects Explore, Solve, or Verify actions.

title: Research
text:
Broader Goal
My long-term goal is to build evaluation settings and learning methods that keep pace with model capability. That means moving away from narrow, static benchmarks and toward systems that are tested under the kinds of ambiguity, noise, and variation they will face in real use.

title: Projects
text: This page highlights research projects, benchmarks, and software I have built or contributed to, grouped by research theme. For code, see GitHub.

title: Projects
text:
Benchmarks and Evaluation


mSCoRe
LREC-COLING 2026 · Code
A multilingual and scalable benchmark for skill-based commonsense reasoning. Evaluates LLMs across 5 languages and 10 reasoning skills with dynamic complexity scaling (L0–L3) that progressively increases task difficulty. Contains 5,600 instances spanning general and social/cultural commonsense.


MedRGB
AAAI 2026 Workshop on AI for Scientific Research · Code
A benchmarking framework for medical retrieval-augmented generation systems. Evaluates across four practical test scenarios — Standard-RAG, Sufficiency, Integration, and Robustness — with varying proportions of signal and noise documents, assessing reliability beyond accuracy alone.

title: Projects
text:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation


ESV-RAG
Research project · Code
Explore–Solve–Verify Reasoning-Augmented Generation with Monte Carlo Tree Search planning. Formulates multi-hop question answering as a planning problem where an agent selects optimal action sequences through PUCT-guided tree search. Achieves state-of-the-art results across multiple FlashRAG benchmark datasets.

title: Projects
text:
Multilingual Learning and Transfer


ChatGPT Beyond English
Findings of EMNLP 2023 · Code
Large-scale zero-shot evaluation of ChatGPT (GPT-3.5-turbo) on 7 NLP tasks across 37 languages spanning high-, medium-, low-, and extremely low-resource settings. Compares ChatGPT against state-of-the-art supervised models to quantify the multilingual gap.


Multilingual Relational Transfer (ZSRL)
Findings of ACL 2023 · Code
Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer for information extraction with multiple source and target languages. Introduces principled language selection via linguistic distance metrics and graph-relational domain adaptation (GrDA), yielding +3.0–3.7 F1 over random source selection.


Language-Disentangle-Distillation
Research project · Code
A compact multilingual pre-trained language model that explicitly disentangles language-specific and language-agnostic representations via knowledge distillation from XLM-RoBERTa. Uses dual hidden-state streams with Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) to address the curse of multilinguality.


Multilingual Federated Distillation
Research project · Code
A federated learning framework for multilingual NLP addressing data heterogeneity (different languages) and latency heterogeneity (different hardware). Combines semi-asynchronous tier training, personalized tokenizers, and federated distillation to reduce per-round communication payload by over 90%.

title: Projects
text:
Domain Adaptation for Information Extraction


DA4JIE
Findings of EMNLP 2022 · Code
Unsupervised domain adaptation for joint information extraction across four tasks simultaneously (entity mention, event trigger, relation, and event argument extraction). Proposes Instance-relational Domain Adaptation (IrDA) with a chain graph topology and Context-invariant Structure Learning (CiSL).


DAA — Domain-specific Adapters for Event Detection
Findings of ACL-IJCNLP 2021 · Code
Unsupervised domain adaptation for event detection using three lightweight bottleneck adapters per BERT layer (source-private, target-private, joint). Combines layer-wise adversarial alignment with Wasserstein sample selection and orthogonality constraints.


MSP-DA
COLING 2022 · Code
Unsupervised domain adaptation for text classification via meta self-paced learning. Integrates MAML-style meta-learning with a neural self-paced learning module that dynamically partitions labeled source data into easy and hard subsets to simulate virtual domain shift during training.


Event Detection with Gumbel-Softmax Context Selection
PAKDD 2020 · Code
Event detection model that learns to select the most relevant context words using the Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax estimator, handling discrete word selection as differentiable hard attention during training. Avoids hand-crafted dependency rules through iterative context updates.

title: Projects
text:
Other Research Projects


Discourse Classification
Research project · Code
Argumentative discourse parsing for student essays. Addresses the 512-token BERT constraint via a Hierarchical Document Encoder (HDE) that splits documents into overlapping segments, classifying discourse elements (Lead, Claim, Evidence, Counterclaim, Rebuttal, Concluding Statement) at the sentence level.

title: Projects
text:
Software and Tools


agent-maker
Software project
A declarative framework for compiling LLM agents from high-level specifications — interactive interview, preset shortcuts, or JSON spec driven. Supports 7 providers (Claude, Gemini, GPT, Perplexity, OpenAI, OpenRouter, GitHub Copilot) across 3 transport layers (browser automation, HTTP API, CLI subprocess) with a unified async runtime and multi-agent monitoring.


Kendo Translation Platform
Software project
A collaborative Japanese–English translation platform for kendo instructional content. Features a MAC-RAG (Multi-Agent Collaborative RAG) AI pipeline with keigo detection, terminology database, multi-candidate generation, quality scoring, and real-time collaborative editing backed by Supabase.
